MANAGEMENT GUIDELINES
I. All parishes are to take special care to be exemplary in the 
arrangement, decoration, beautification and maintenance of 
the parish facilities and premises.
II. No areas in the parish premises should be in a state of 
disrepair or constitute an eye - sore.
III. Arrangements should be in place for regular and thorough 
cleaning of the facilities
IV. As much as possible, plants are to be used to beautify the 
premises so as to create a physical sense of beauty and appeal 
that could help attune the minds of worshippers to the 
spiritual.
V. Depending on the particular state of the parish, a 
maintenance and cleaning plan should be in pace which will 
be audited regularly.
VI. Each parish is to develop clear guidelines on the use of 
facilities in order to promote order, inclusion and avoid 
scandal.
1) Health and Safety
I. The church is committed to the Health and safety of 
all within its premises. Each parish is to have a 
system of ensuring that the premises are safe by 
controlling and eliminating hazards in and around 
the church thereby preventing injuries and ill health.
II. Safety signs and instructions must be displayed 
conspicuously in strategic places and everyone 
educated to abide by them.
III. Each parish is to have guidelines on health, safety 
and security and all staff and parishioners educated 
on measures in cases of emergencies and threats.
IV. Regular risk assessments should be carried out and 
necessary adjustments and updates made and 
communicated to all responsible parties in 
conjunction with the Security & Traffic Management 
Committee.
2) Buildings
I. The church buildings shall be such as to provide easy 
access for the handicapped and physically 
challenged who may be on wheel chairs.
II. The doors and windows should be wide in order to 
provide easy escape in case of emergencies or fire 
that may cause the congregation to run out of the 
church.
III. Arrangement of pews should be such as to allow easy 
and orderly movements of congregation and enhance 
the aesthetic view of the church.
IV. Generally, the building plan should take into 
consideration the health and safety of all concerned. 
Materials used in the building must not be hazardous
3) Fire Extinguishers
I. Fire extinguishers should be located at strategic 
points within the church buildings and must not be 
removed except at times of emergency or to carry out 
4039
maintenance on them.
II. Certain staff including security men should be 
trained on the use of fire extinguishers. The 
extinguishers must be serviced periodically and 
ensure that they are in good condition.
4) First Aid.
I. The church should provide first aid kits in marked locations 
within the buildings and some personnel (including security 
men) be trained in the administering of first aid.
II. Whenever the content of the box is used, a written report 
shall be made by the security officer on duty and forwarded 
to the Chairman of the Security & Traffic Management 
Committee and the Parish Priest within 24 hours. A 
designated and competent person should check the box 
periodically.
5) Electrical wiring and equipment
I. The electrical wirings must be professionally done to 
prevent exposure of naked wires that may constitute safety 
hazards. Use of extension cables that may result to tripping 
hazards must be avoided. No alterations is to be made to the 
electrical wirings without due approval and in accordance to 
the Maintenance committee policies.
II. Electrical appliances and equipment must be maintained, 
inspected and tested regularly and records (log book) 
maintained by the Church maintenance officer. Generally, 
electrical appliances should be used safely following 
manufacturer's instructions and overloading of sockets 
avoided.
6) Chemicals
I. Hazardous chemicals e.g. cleaning or pest control chemicals 
must be kept in a locked environment with restricted access.
II. Ensure that the personnel who have to use the hazardous 
chemicals are aware of the necessary precautions in the event 
of an emergency involving the substances.
7) Maintenance and repairs
I. Areas of the church under renovation or major repairs should 
be condoned off where possible and clearly marked. 
Contractors working within the premises must adhere to the 
use of notices and instructions warning of possible dangers.
II. Proper housekeeping during and after any maintenance or 
repairs must be enforced.
III. Contractors used for renovation and maintenance must be 
competent and professional in order to reduce the possibility 
of use of inferior materials that may constitute a health and 
Safety hazard.
4241
PROCUREMENT AND CONTRACTING GUIDELINES
I. The overall objectives of the procurement and contracting 
guidelines are to ensure that these processes do not result in 
scandal or acrimony, as far as possible and result in the best 
outcome for the parishes in terms of cost, time, customer 
service and effort.
II. Each parish, according to its particular circumstances and 
guided by Archdiocesan norms is to establish a scale of 
purchasing limits and guidelines for approvals and 
procedures for contracting services on behalf of the parish.
III. Each parish is to develop and maintain lists of routinely 
purchased items, their usual quantities, prices and a 
minimum of three vendors for each item.
IV. A system of purchase requisition and payment should be 
established and maintained according to the local needs of 
the parish.
V. Where purchase orders exceed a previously set limit by the 
finance council, a competitive bidding system that utilizes 
the parish bulletin should be used. Only credible vendors 
with a track record for their business and ethical business 
practices should be shortlisted for bids. Conflict of interest 
situations are to be avoided.
VI. Payments should only be made on completion of the supply 
or job order.
VII. However, for projects of large amounts as established by the 
Parish Finance Council, advance payment guarantees are to 
be used to protect the interest of the church.
VIII. For large projects such as building, renovations, and supply 
of heavy equipment, legal contracts are to be used for awards 
using the services of legal practitioners on the Parish Finance 
Council and other volunteers where conflict of interest 
situations exist.
IX. Each parish is to develop a purchasing process that is cost 
effective for the parishes and suppliers. A well-planned 
purchasing process will ensure that policies are followed, 
pitfalls are avoided and a successful outcome is achieved. It 
is important to be aware of responsibilities and to plan the 
entire purchase process before starting. Errors, uncertainty 
and unrealistic timelines can undermine market confidence, 
discredit a purchasing process and devalue the outcome of 
the procurement.
Standards for Parish Office Services
I. In order to create a welcoming parish environment, the 
parish office is often the point of contact for new comers and 
parishioners outside of Masses. Special care should be taken 
to make the physical space attractive, clean and welcoming.
II. Processes to provide services such as counseling, collection 
of documents, registrations, payments and enquiries should 
be made simple, efficient and offered in a prompt and 
pleasant manner. The same standards should exist in 
bookshop, clinic services etc.
III. Personnel should be well trained to understand and exhibit 
the evangelisation nature of the services that they render.
IV. Standards on timeliness, welcoming and cheerful customer 
service, courtesies, professional conduct and interactions, 
exemplary Christian behaviour – not showing favouritism or 
discrimination, should be set, monitored and form part of the 
appraisal process.
4443
GUIDELINES ON BUILDING AND RENOVATION 
PROJECTS MANAGEMENT
I. When considering a building or renovation project, the 
highest priority should always be the care of those whom we 
serve.
II. When a parish embarks on a building program it should set 
priorities on the types of facilities to be constructed to insure 
the pastoral plan of the parish is addressed. The right order, 
placing matters of salvation and eternity before temporal 
matters and placing the basic needs and rights of people 
before things and places, should guide the parish in its 
decision making. Parishes are to be guided by Pope Francis's 
admonition to be a poor church for the poor.
Policy on General Procedural Requirements of the Parish for 
Construction
I. All parishes and other archdiocesan entities must follow the 
following steps in planning for and accomplishing all new 
construction, additions, alterations, remodelling and 
renovation projects with a budget of =N500, 000 or more or 
that require the services of an architect or engineer.
II. These projects must comply with all local building codes, 
regulations and ordinances. The contractor must have 
appropriate skill and experience for the project, must 
warranty his work and carry liability insurance and workers 
compensation.
III. The parish priest or the administrator of the entity involved 
should be the person to pursue all necessary approvals at the 
Archdiocesan offices once the need has been established. 
The Parish Finance Council will oversee professionals to 
present building plans and budgets for scrutiny and approval 
based on a competitive bidding process advertised in the 
bulletin and through word of mouth and referrals.
IV. A building committee should be set up and should be made 
up of parishioners with the necessary expertise and 
character. An orientation on the code of ethics and conduct 
will be administered.
V. The building committee should establish procedures and 
processes to guide the execution of the building project in 
agreement with the parish priest and the parish finance 
council.
VI. The building committee is a sub- committee of the PFC and 
is responsible for ensuring that all necessary approvals and 
permits from Government and other authorities are in place 
before the commencement of the project.
VII. Parishioners are to be updated on the progress of the project 
on a quarterly basis, at the minimum by a delegate of the 
building committee.
VIII. The building committee chairman must sign all documents 
sent to the Parish Finance Council for recommendation for 
payment of contractors.
IX. The Archbishop is to be given a quarterly update of the 
building project through the Archdiocesan Land/Building 
Commission.
Legal compliance and risk management
I. All parishes in the Archdiocese are to ensure that all 
activities and ventures are guided by legal counsel using the 
pro – bono services of legal practitioners in the parish 
finance council and in the parishes or deaneries.

INTRODUCTION TO REVISED EDITION
To fulfill her missionary mandate “Go forth and make Disciples of 
all Nations” (Matt 28:19), the Church, after the Vatican II Council, 
has adopted a clarion call for co-responsibility and collaboration 
among all Christ's Faithful; such that all members of the Church – 
Clergy, Religious and Laity – according to their proper charisms and 
roles collaborate in the task of fulfilling her mission. This co-
responsibility and collaboration find concrete expression in the 
community of God's people otherwise called the Parish. As a 
privileged place for Catholics seeking to deepen their relationship 
with God, the Parish therefore becomes a place where: the journey of 
faith begins and is nourished; faith is renewed, and members grow 
into communities. The faith of members grows in response to their 
encounter with God which in turn makes them passionate about the 
proclamation of the Gospel. 
To realize this vision and meet the evangelical demands of our 
changing religious landscape, there is great need to build spiritually-
committed, pastorally-engaged and growth-oriented Parishes 
wherein everybody is actively involved in the affairs of the Parish 
and even lukewarm parishioners are reintegrated into the living body 
of Christ. Indeed, as advocated by the Second Vatican Council, the 
parish should be a product of shared responsibility and effective 
collaboration the clergy and lay faithful. 
The Parish Councils – Pastoral, Laity and Finance – have been set up 
with this objective. Their authenticity and effectiveness depend on 
fidelity to the mission of Christ and the Church, the aim of each 
council is to make the life and activity of the parish ever more closely 
conformed to the gospel. It is to this end that the Archdiocese of 
Lagos has come up with the Second Edition of the Parish Guidelines 
for Pastoral, Laity and Finance Councils as well the administration 
of the Parish. The goals of the guidelines remain as enumerated in 
the First Edition:
·
· Strengthen the functionality of existing parish programs, 
structures and organizations,
· Foster collaborative approaches that maximize parish 
resources and put temporal goods of the Church to more 
effective use,
· Re-establish an evangelizing perspective for all pastoral 
ministries and reorient parish activities from maintenance 
to mission,
· Bridge social and structural divides, and build a 
community where everyone is welcome and has a sense of 
belonging,
· And most importantly, build a spiritually-committed and 
more engaged parish. 
As our society evolves and in the face of new horizons and 
possibilities, there is the need to continually improve our response as 
a community of God's people. Therefore, as promised, this Revised 
Edition is the fruit of reviews and observations drawn from the 
experience of living out the Pastoral Guidelines since 
implementation began in 2015 as well as in consideration of the 
current situation of things in our Church and world. 
Let me at this point acknowledge with gratitude, the efforts of the 
Priests who have been faithful in implementing the provisions of 
these Guidelines in their parishes since it came into effect in 2015. 
Comments from their different pastoral experiences, areas of 
challenges and points of concerns during the implementation thus 
far have gone a long way to enrich us towards reviewing the 
Guidelines. We also appreciate and commend the efforts of the Lay 
Use strategic methods to set parish priorities,
05 06
Faithful in seeing to the successful running of the Councils in their 
faithfulness to the Guidelines. 
As we did in the First Edition, in developing these Guidelines, great 
care was taken to ensure they align with the Teaching  of the Church, 
the provisions of the Canon Law, stipulations of the Second Vatican 
Council, and recommendations of the Catholic Bishops Conference 
of Nigeria on matters pertaining thereto. More so, these Revised 
Parish Guidelines are in no way a radical departure from the first 
edition but a revised and improved continuity with the former. 
It is our sincere hope that in the years ahead, the continued 
implementation of these Revised Guidelines will “enable the 
proclamation of Christ to reach people, mold communities, and have 
a deep and incisive influence in bringing Gospel values to bear in 
society and culture” (Pope John Paul II, Novo Millennio Ineunte, 
29). Also, it is our prayer that they will further assist in fostering 
mutual relationships between the clergy and the Laity. Working 
together, Clergy and Lay leaders will continually seek new and 
creative methods to live fully the Christian mission in the context of 
our contemporary society. 
Indeed, all of us can look toward the future with the hope that the 
Guidelines will spur parishes to attain greater vitality and vibrancy. 
To bring this hope to fruition, I encourage all Parish Priests/Priests-
in-Charge to remain committed to the implementation of these 
Guidelines to address the varied aspects of parish life. I also 
encourage the Lay faithful to continue to lend their gifts and talents 
to the building up of their parishes as communities of prayer and 
worship, communities of faith and service. 
While we are grateful for the sincere and widespread acceptance of 
the First Edition, I earnestly exhort the Parish Priests and Priests-in-
Charge of parishes, with the help of the Religious and Lay Faithful, 
to consolidate on the gains of the implementation of the First 
Edition, I entrust this Second Edition to the pastoral zeal of Parish 
Priests/Priests-in-Charge in the hope that its application may 
produce abundant fruit for the growth of the parishes and our 
Archdiocese as a whole. 
We look forward to the years ahead with trusting optimism, but 
without underestimating the problems we face. Indeed, as the years 
pass by and in the light of new issues, these guidelines remain work 
in progress to continually address contemporary concerns with the 
unchanging truths of the Gospel. 
In keeping with my pastoral responsibilities, as the Chief Shepherd 
of the Archdiocese of Lagos, I hereby approve “The Parish 
Guidelines – Second Edition” to be effective in all the Parishes, 
Quasi-Parishes, Outstations and Chaplaincies in the Archdiocese of 
Lagos beginning with the 2020 Liturgical Year. 
As the Chief Shepherd, I willingly offer you my support, spiritual 
solicitude and assurance of prayers. We entrust the implementation 
of this Second Edition to the maternal care of the Blessed Virgin 
Mary, the Mother of the Church. Through her intercession, may 
these Guidelines bear lasting fruits in our lives and our Archdiocese.  
+ Alfred Adewale Martins
Archbishop of Lagos

PART A: GENERAL BACKGROUND
The main goal of the Lagos Archdiocesan Human Resource 
Management Manual is to establish guidelines and create an 
enabling environment for the effective development of spiritually – 
committed parishioners through engaged parishes.
The purpose of this manual is to provide general guidelines, in 
cognizance of modern management best practices, the Code of 
Canon and Gospel values for the human resource management of all 
who work in and for the Catholic Archdiocese of Lagos, clergy, 
religious and lay.
1) These guidelines aim to achieve a culture of ethics, 
accountability, openness and confidence in leadership, 
particularly at the parish level. The resultant effectiveness 
and efficiency in the running of parishes would create a 
welcoming environment that would encourage more 
parishioners to participate more actively in parish activities, 
thus growing spiritually – committed parishioners and 
engaged parishes.
2) The Church is an Institution and an Employer: The Local 
Ordinary collaborates with the clergy who exercise their 
ministry in his diocese, as well as employs non-clergy 
workers, while parish priests employ parish workers, 
including volunteers. The Catholic Church in the 
Archdiocese of Lagos wishes to distinguish itself by 
ensuring that in her role as an employer and teacher, it 
upholds the following basic Christian Principles for Church 
Human Resources Management:
a. Dignity of the Human Person: All people are 
created in God's image and likeness. The dignity of 
each person, realized in community with others, is 
the criterion with which all ethical, economic, social, 
and relational aspects are to be measured. The 
following questions ought to be constant points of 
reflections in our interactions with others – Is that 
dignity protected or undermined? Is the working 
environment one that promotes mutual respect and 
trust?
b. Dignity of Labor: As articulated in John Paul II's 
encyclical, Laborem Exercens (On Human Work), 
“work is a good thing for people – a good thing for 
humankind – because through work people not only 
transform nature, but also achieve fulfilment as 
human beings and in a sense become more fully 
human. Indeed, it is through work that people 
become co-creators with God in transforming the 
world.”
c. Collegiality: Persons within the church have 
different roles and functions, talents, skills, and 
abilities to be developed and shared. All are enjoined 
to cooperate in a spirit of trust and generosity. 
Decisions ought to be made with as much 
consultation and collaboration as appropriate.
d. Subsidiarity: Individuals need to participate in 
decision-making processes about their work 
situations and not have such decisions made without 
appropriate consultation. Decisions should be made 
at the lowest appropriate level. This principle has to 
be balanced with a proper respect for the common 
1009
good and for the authority responsible for the broader 
mission of the church.
e. Individual Responsibility (Autonomy) and 
Initiative: Each person should enjoy the right to act 
freely, conscientiously, and responsibly without 
undue force. As with all freedom, this right is 
accompanied by corresponding responsibility and 
accountability.
f. Stewardship of Resources: A balanced, mutually 
beneficial work relationship needs to exist between 
the parish in its role as employer and its workforce. 
Parishioners voluntarily contribute the financial 
resources for needed services and support provided 
by the parish. Parish leaders and staff must 
demonstrate accountability for the human, capital, 
and financial resources given to the parish.
g. Equal Opportunity Employer: It is the policy of 
the Archdiocese to promote equal opportunity in the 
areas of recruitment, employment, training, 
development, and promotion where appropriate. 
Employment in the Archdiocese and subsequent 
development or promotion will go to those 
individuals most qualified by education for the 
positions offered without regard to gender, age, 
disability or ethnicity, except where religion or 
gender is a bona fide occupational qualification as it 
is for ministerial positions.
3) Archdiocesan Organization Structure:
Below is a representation of the organization structure of the 
Lagos Archdiocese.

I. In view of the number of priests in the Archdiocese

THE CORE GOALS OF THE GUIDELINES
The parish priest is the key driver of the parish environment and 
culture, which in turn reflects on the level of parish engagement. The 
clergy of the Lagos Archdiocese is therefore the most critical 
resource in growing spiritually – committed parishioners and 
engaged parishes. The Lagos Archdiocese, having considered the 
current ratio of clergy to the laity in the Archdiocese and wishing to 
significantly improve on the levels of parish engagement and 
spiritual commitment of parishioners provide these guidelines with 
the following prime objectives:
I. To maintain the integrity and dignity of the clergy through 
adequate, consistent and fair remuneration and conditions of 
service, irrespective of postings.
II. To assist the clergy with his duties as the leader of the 
community through the establishment of efficient and 
effective management processes and personnel.
III. To equip the clergy with effective leadership and people 
management skills for effective delegation and oversight of 
parish activities.
IV. To establish parish performance measures that engender 
collaboration and focus on spiritual growth, worship, service 
and community building outcomes.
V. To preserve and live the Catholic faith
1) Introduction
The priesthood is not a profession, but a vocation. Yet, all who are 
called to work in the vineyard of the Lord must be adequately 
prepared for the duties and challenges of this calling, which is a 
unique ministry. The preparation for an effective priestly ministry 
starts with an adequate seminary education and formation.
2) Posting and Placement of Priests
I. To enhance pastoral effectiveness, posting/placement of 
priests in the Archdiocese of Lagos (whether diocesan 
priests, priests belonging to Institutes of Consecrated Life 
and Societies of Apostolic life, Fidei Donum priests, priests 
on sabbatical, student priests, or priests belonging to 
personal prelatures, etc.) will be executed after a 
careful process of discernment and consultations 
aimed at finding the best - fit clergy; this should be in 
consideration of the clergy's talents, skills, spiritual 
gifts and charisma as well as the pastoral needs and 
characteristics of the Archdiocese, parishes and 
institutions.
II. Fidei Donum priests will be required to have definite 
periods of attachment on resumption in the Archdiocese and 
may be granted only one renewal of postings, subject to the 
needs of the Archdiocese and the priest's performance and 
availability. This is to avoid the potential risk of staying too 
long in the Archdiocese without being incardinated and too 
long away from his mother diocese and losing touch with 
home. Also, Priests on sabbatical and student priests would 
have their stay in the Archdiocese defined in a formal letter.
III. To ensure stability, effectiveness and fulfilment, postings for 
all priests in the Archdiocese will be for defined periods of 
time to be stipulated at posting, under normal circumstances.
1615
·
extension of not more than (3) three years);
· Two (2) years for assistant parish priests (non-
renewable);
· Three (3) years for Fidei Donum priests (with a 
possible extension of not more than two years).
Notwithstanding the above, it remains the prerogative of the 
Archbishop to decide how long a Priest stays at a particular 
posting.
IV. The parish priest or administrator of an Archdiocesan 
institution is to ensure that an inventory of the parish or 
institution's belongings are kept and updated and that 
inventory of the personal belongings of all residing in the 
parish residences are kept and updated.
3) Performance Management of Priests
I. The clergy is encouraged to have quarterly auto guided 
assessments of their performance in their assigned postings. 
An expert ad-hoc committee should be set up to work out the 
modalities for this performance management process. In this 
regard:
II. Priests are encouraged to have periodic (annual, at the 
minimum) reviews of canons 528-537 which clearly spell 
out the pastoral and administrative duties of priests.
III. Priests are encouraged to have regular reviews and adhere to 
the canon recommendations on their way of life and dressing 
in order to preserve the dignity of the office and command 
the necessary respect from the lay faithful needed to be 
effective in their ministry.
Five (5) years for Parish Priests (with a possible IV. The deans or superiors of institutions will carry out bi- 
annual auto – guided performance appraisals on pre – 
determined criteria on the priestly life as well as the duties of 
the specific office, making recommendations to the 
Chancery.
V. Parishes are encouraged to conduct annual or bi – annual 
evaluations to monitor their progress in growing parish 
engagement, spiritual – commitment and effective parish 
councils. These will form part of parish progress reports 
reviewed by the Archbishop or his delegate.
VI. Discipline is critical to achieving the mission of the Church. 
Sanctions will be exercised as appropriate, always in the 
spirit of love in order to foster a healthy love and fear of 
authority.
VII. Correcting erring parishioners with love and understanding 
is a requirement from parish priests in the archdiocese in 
order to prevent unhealthy loyalty. Adequate care is to be 
taken to avoid actual or perceived punishment of dissenting 
views from parishioners and staff.
4) On-going Formation and Education
I. The clergy are encouraged to pursue their spiritual wellbeing 
and development with utmost care and diligence using all 
available opportunities and in accordance with the Code of 
Canon Law and diocesan injunctions.
II. All newly ordained priests and priests posted to Lagos 
Diocese will undergo an orientation programme within the 
first year of their posting.
III. The clergy are required to develop and execute an annual 
1817
personal improvement plan in order to attain adequate and 
continuous perfection of their knowledge of things divine 
and human. In this way, they will prepare themselves to enter 
with greater advantage into dialogue with their 
contemporaries. “Presbyterorum Ordinis” says it all: 
“Secular culture and even sacred science are advancing at an 
unprecedented rate in our time”.
IV. The above can be achieved through:
a) Self-study of appropriate and relevant books, 
periodicals, journals, research works, audios, videos, 
webinars, webcasts, etc.
b) Study groups to address and review specific issues of 
interest to the Archdiocese.
c) Programmed attendance at relevant seminars, 
workshops and conferences on spiritual and religious 
continuous formation and education, pastoral and 
leadership development programs.
V. Members of the clergy are also encouraged to regularly 
utilize discernment and self reflection processes to identify 
and develop their unique talents and gifts in order to most 
effectively serve God and those God has placed in their care. 
All are called to be pastors, yet each is endowed with one or 
more of the following ministerial gifts:
a) 'Apostle': establishes and builds churches.
b) Prophet: points to the future and points out sin.
c) Evangelist: brings people into the body of Christ to 
be “discipled”.
d) Pastor: stay, oversee, nurture and guide
e) Teacher: lays the foundation by digging into the 
tight, dark places, shining light and picking apart the 
Word of Truth.
VI. With the permission of the Archbishop, the clergy can 
embark on sabbatical leave for further academic or cultural 
studies with durations ranging from three months to one 
year, based on individual talents, the needs of the 
Archdiocese and availability of resources.
VII. As a guide, priests are encouraged to apply for sabbatical 
leave within the first ten years of ordination and thereafter, 
every ten years.
VIII. The duration of such sabbatical will vary based on the 
objective, the nature and availability.
IX. Such sabbaticals could be academic or cultural.
X. Sponsorships for studies and sabbaticals will be funded by 
the Archdiocese from a dedicated Archdiocesan Clergy 
Study Fund established and funded from generous 
donations from Catholics and other people of goodwill.
5) Remuneration - Monthly Allowance
I. Just as “the worker deserves to be paid” (Lk 10:7), “The Lord 
ordered, likewise, that those announcing the Gospel live 
from the Gospel” (1 Cor. 9:14), and Presbyterorum Ordinis 
expatiates that “Taking into consideration the condition of 
different places and times as well as the nature of the office 
they hold, the remuneration to be received by each of the 
Priests should be fundamentally the same for all living in the 
same circumstances” (Vatican Council II, D 63.20).
II. The Lagos Archdiocese will ensure that every priest receives 
an appropriate monthly allowance that will be determined 
and reviewed on a regular basis to ensure it is reflective of 
current situations. The Priests Welfare Committee is to 
recommend further on this. The amount should empower the 
priest to take care of his basic personal and pastoral needs 
2019
such that he is not dependent on the goodwill of parishioners 
and the resources of the parish thereby empowering him to 
be an unbiased pastor of the souls entrusted to his care.
III. For purposes of accountability and protection of the good 
name of priests, the Chancery should make this amount and 
the items it covers known to the parish finance councils.
IV. Parishes differ in their ability to cater for the needs of the 
clergy in their service. Consequently, while proper parish 
accounting systems are being put in place, parishes that 
cannot afford the determined amount can be paired with 
those that can, who will transfer such amounts to the needy 
parishes.
6) Remuneration - Benefits
I. Accommodation: Priests will be provided accommodation 
with their assignments by the institutions they have been 
posted to or as directed in the posting letter. As far as the 
logistics allow, priests are encouraged to live in communities 
with fellow clergy and show examples of Christian 
brotherhood through their way of life.
II. Car: At ordination and following a process of consultation, 
each newly ordained priest is to be presented with a car in 
accordance with the Archdiocesan policy and standards 
current at the time by either the natural family, parish family 
or deanery family. Care should be taken to ensure that only 
one car is presented and that each ordained priest is presented 
with one. Each parish will arrange for the maintenance and 
fuelling of the priest's car for pastoral duties.
III. Annual Leave: Every Priest is eligible for an annual leave of 
one calendar month, with payment of the normal monthly 
allowance, plus the current Archdiocesan leave allowance.
IV. Medical: The Archdiocese will provide medical care for its 
priests either through medical insurance or directly through 
hospitals. Efforts to procure a viable medical/health 
insurance scheme to cover all Priests in the Archdiocese are 
ongoing and will be put in place. The parishes or institutions 
to which the Priests are assigned will pay the premium for 
such insurance scheme. Until such a time that the above 
reference scheme is effectively put in place, every parish in 
the Archdiocese is to determine a suitable hospital for the 
medical care of the clergy in their service. All the bills 
including an annual mandatory medical check – up, will be 
paid for by the parish or another assigned parish where the 
home parish cannot afford it.
V. Physical/Social Wellbeing: In keeping with the adage 
“mens sana in corpore sano”, priests are encouraged to be 
involved in one form of sporting activity or the other e.g. 
lawn tennis, table tennis, basketball, volleyball and football. 
Indoor games like chess, scrabble, monopoly, ludo and 
draught are also encouraged. Inter-deanery competitions can 
be organized in these sporting activities. Furthermore, 
occasional get-together over lunch or dinner with brother 
priests should be encouraged. Each deanery is to have a 
coordinating priest for these activities.
7) Remuneration - Retirement:
I. The Archdiocese will ensure the adequate care of all retired 
priests in either one of special retirement homes for the 
clergy or one of the designated retirement parishes in the 
diocese.
II. A special diocesan committee will oversee the planning and 
execution of additional two retirement homes for priests in 
2221
the next five years as well as the immediate identification of 
suitable parishes to be designated for the residence of retired 
priests.
III. The cost of caring for the priests in designated retirement 
parishes will be borne by the designated parishes while the 
costs incurred in running/maintaining the retirement homes 
will be borne by the Archdiocese.
8) Gifts
I. Priests receive gifts after performing certain pastoral 
services and out of the goodwill of parishioners, friends and 
associates. The laity should be guided by “Presbyterorum 
Ordinis” which affirms the need for “a proper standard of 
living” for Priests, while reminding them that, “as men 
whose “portion and inheritance” is the Lord, they ought to 
use temporal goods only for those purposes to which the 
teaching of Christ and the direction of the Church allow them 
to be devoted”, warning that “Priests and Bishops alike are to 
avoid everything that might in any way antagonize 
(scandalize) the poor and “are to put aside all appearance of 
vanity in their surroundings”.
II. The lay faithful should exercise care and good judgment as to 
the kind and value of gifts they give to Priests. In all 
situations however, the intention of the donor of a gift to a 
priest should be respected.
III. In accord with Chapter V, Canon, 1267.1 and 2, priests are 
reminded that unless the contrary is established, offerings 
given to superiors or administrators of any ecclesiastical 
juridic person, even a private one, are assumed given to the 
juridic person itself. The offerings cannot be refused except 
for a just cause. Where grounds for a just cause are suspected, 
the Archbishop is to be consulted before refusal.
IV. Additionally, Code of Canon Law, Chapter 111, Canon 283 
(1) requires clerics to foster simplicity of life and to refrain 
from all things that have a semblance of vanity.
V. Canon 283 (11) further directs the clergy on the appropriate 
use of gifts or goods acquired as a result of their exercise of 
the ecclesiastical office and which are left over after the 
provision of their decent support and the fulfilment of all the 
duties of their own state. They are to be used for the good of 
the church and works of charity.
VI. The clergy is enjoined to desist from soliciting for personal 
gifts in cash and kind from the lay faithful.
VII. The clergy is advised to direct special challenges they may be 
facing requiring financial support to the Archbishop.
9) The Relationship of the Clergy with Family Members
I. Members of the clergy have been called to a life of sacrifice 
and dedication to the service of God. Due to the economic 
and social conditions of Nigeria, some members of the clergy 
are burdened with family members who look up to them for 
financial and social support.
II. However, the relatives of the clergy should not live with the 
priest in the parish, except for short visits.
III. The priest is to assist his family members as a matter of 
charity and not of sense of duty. However, when there is a 
grave need of assistance from the immediate family of the 
priest, he should be able to get help from the Priests' Welfare 
Committee or the Archdiocese.
2423
10) Protection of Children and Sexual Abuse
I. Children are precious gifts from God, with an inherent right 
to dignity of life and bodily integrity, which must be 
respected and protected by all. With regard to human 
sexuality and sexual abuse, it is of critical importance that 
every priest should have handy, and from time to time re-
read, the document “ Called to Love “, issued by the Catholic 
Bishops Conference of Nigeria, and published by the 
Catholic Secretariat of Nigeria, in 2006. It sets out the ethical 
standards for Clergy and Seminarians in Nigeria in matters 
of sexuality, sexual abuse and sexual perversion.
II. For the avoidance of doubt and to secure the Church against 
claims arising from immoral, criminal and illegal actions of 
Church personnel, the Catholic Bishops of Nigeria consider 
it necessary that clergy, and possibly all those working for 
the Church, should sign a document clearing the Church of 
any liability for their offences.
11) Conspiracy and Blackmail
I. It is a fact of life that from time to time, intentions and actions 
of a priest are either misunderstood or deliberately 
misconstrued with the aim to blackmail. Therefore, 
whenever conspiracy or blackmail is suspected, the dean of 
the deanery to which the parish belongs should constitute a 
panel to thoroughly investigate the allegation(s) with a view 
to unveiling the truth. The resultant report should be duly 
submitted to the dean for review and possible escalation to 
the Archbishop, as appropriate.
12) Death
a. The Death of a Priest
I. The death of a Priest, whether before or after 
retirement, is a great loss to the Church and to his 
immediate family. The Archdiocese will arrange for 
his funeral, with full information to and in 
collaboration with his family, in full recognition of 
the dignity of the priesthood. The Archdiocese will 
commence efforts to secure areas of existing 
cemeteries and/or procuring a dedicated cemetery 
for Priests.
II. Any personal belongings of the deceased Priest will 
be disposed of in accordance with his written will. 
All priests are encouraged to write a will to obviate 
any possible and embarrassing situation in the family 
after his death.
III. Priests are enjoined to keep and update inventories of 
their personal belongings and wills. Items not listed 
on the inventory of personal belongings are deemed 
to belong to the parish or institution.
b. The Death of a Priest's Parent
I. Although a priest leaves his parents to give his life 
fully to the ministry of the church, he still retains 
strong emotional attachment to them. Consequently, 
the loss of any of them weighs heavily on the priest. 
Therefore, it is expected that brother priests should 
show sympathy and solidarity with the bereaved 
priest, ensuring attendance at the funeral of the 
deceased parent.
2625
PART C: LAITY HUMAN RESOURCES MANAGEMENT 
AND WELFARE
1) Introduction
It surely is unusual to talk of "Human Resource 
Management" of the lay workers of the Archdiocese of 
Lagos and its parishes. Traditionally, Priests have engaged 
most of such workers from among relatives, friends and 
parishioners, often as a way of helping them. That was, and 
is, probably, not a bad idea. However, the times have 
changed, and we have to, progressively, move towards 
formalizing these employment relationships, in line with the 
Christian principles enunciated in the introductory part of 
this manual.
2) Types of Employment
a. Regular/Full Time Employee is one who is employed for 
indefinite period and works at least forty (40) hours a week 
and is entitled to full employee compensation and benefits as 
specified by the parish.
b. Regular/Part Time Employee is one who is employed for 
an indefinite period but who is required to work less than 
forty (40) hours a week and is entitled to prorated 
compensation and benefits as may be specified by the parish.
c. Temporary Employee is one who is employed to work for a 
definite period of time on a specific task, and is only entitled 
to benefits as specified under the Employee Compensation 
Act.
d. Volunteers are not employees either of the Archdiocese or 
parishes, but they provide a variety of desirable services that 
1 
would otherwise have been paid for by the Church. An 
occasional get-together with these volunteers to appreciate 
and encourage them would be a good Human Resource 
Management practice.
3) Job Descriptions1
 In the course of these guidelines on employment, wherever 
“Church” is indicated, please read and understand 
“Archdiocese or Parish”.
I. Job descriptions are essential to providing a structure within 
which an employee can perform, and are thus essential to a 
valid performance management system. A job description 
provides the essential agreement as to what functions and 
duties a person holding a particular position will perform.
II. All parishes and institutions are required to develop job 
descriptions ideally before the incumbent is hired for the 
position. If a job description is developed while the job is 
filled, care should be taken to write the description to reflect 
what the job should include, and not simply how the 
incumbent performs the job.
III. An employee of the Archdiocese is not to be in doubt as to 
what is expected of him/her. Consequently, every employee 
must have a functional job description of the duties and 
responsibilities of the job given to him/her.
4) Recruitment and Selection
The Church is committed to selecting and employing the best 
- qualified individuals for positions consistent with the 
Church's long-term best interest.
2827
1
 In the course of these guidelines on employment, wherever “Church” is indicated, please read and 
understand “Archdiocese or Parish”.
a) Advertisement
I. For transparency and the potential opportunity to 
select the best available candidate to fill a vacant 
position, it is advised that vacancies in the 
Archdiocese and parish levels be advertised.
II. Advertisements should be descriptive enough so that 
applicants can exclude themselves if they are not 
qualified, and so that the position seems attractive to 
those who are qualified.
III. The advert should include a brief description of the 
job duties, the qualifications, information about how 
to apply, and any other important information that 
must be communicated to all potential applicants.
IV. It would be more beneficial and cost effective to 
announce parish vacancies in church and/or 
advertise them in parish bulletins/notice boards. 
However, they can also be advertised in relevant 
daily newspapers if considered appropriate in the 
circumstance.
b) Application Forms
I. Application forms are used to gather information 
from an applicant prior to selecting applicants for an 
interview. An application form should be designed to 
fulfill several different purposes:
a. It should gather identifying and contact 
information from the applicant for later 
reference.
b. It should gather information on the 
applicant's qualifications and educational 
background, including past employment 
opportunities, so that those can be compared 
to the job description.
c. It should gather information necessary for 
checking references for the applicant.
d. It should provide a method for applicants to 
describe their qualifications in a way that 
makes it apparent whether they are qualified 
for the position.
II. Application forms must be simple, clear, easy to 
understand and must specify when and where to be 
submitted.
III. The Office of the Archdiocesan Human Resource 
Management to guide parishes in this regard will 
issue a template.
c) Interviewing
I. Whatever the job may be, a panel interview is 
recommended to reduce interviewer bias and 
subjectivity. For all hiring at the parish level 
however, the Parish Priest or his delegate will be 
involved in some form or fashion.
d) Background Checks/Referees
I. Background checks on those that are to be hired in 
the Archdiocese is recommended not because there is 
any reason to mistrust employees or volunteers but 
because the Church has a definite responsibility to 
ensure overall suitability (ecclesial and otherwise) of 
persons for specific positions. These background 
checks are very important in our contemporary 
times. Certain parishioners can be involved as 
appropriate.
3029
e) Pre-employment Fitness for Duty Tests
I. Offers for employment must be contingent upon the 
candidates satisfying the requirements in pre-
employment tests (medical and other tests as may be 
appropriate, taking care to avoid areas that infringe 
on the rights and dignity of individuals).
II. Whenever there is a doubt, it is advisable to err on the 
side of caution and clarify from the archdiocesan 
human resource office.
f) Employment Offers
I. A provisional offer of employment may be made to 
the successful candidate subject to a proper contract 
of employment.
II. Such offer of employment should contain the job 
title, type of employment (i.e. regular, part-time etc), 
what the offer is contingent upon, terms and 
conditions, and space to indicate acceptance and 
agreeable start date.
5) Conditions of Employment
a) Probation
I. A regular full-time employee of the Church will be 
employed for a probationary period of six months, 
starting on the date of employment. During this 
probationary period, both the Church and the 
employee are free to end the employment 
relationship without cause. Either party seeking to 
end the employment within the probationary period 
would give a two - week notice or payment of salary 
in lieu thereof.
II. At the end of the probationary period, the Church 
may do any of the following:
a. Confirm the employee's appointment in 
writing, which will automatically convert his 
or her status to that of a regular full-time 
employee;
b. End the employment relationship as 
contained in the above paragraph;
c. Extend the probationary period with written 
notice for up to an additional three months.
III. During the probationary period, and any extension of 
it, other provisions of employment can be extended 
to or withheld from the employee at the Church's 
discretion.
b) Hours of Work
I. Subject to possible local flexibility in parishes, the 
general hours of work for employees in the 
Archdiocese shall be from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., Monday 
to Friday, with a one - hour lunch break.
II. The general norm of a forty-hour workweek shall 
guide the deployment of employees whose schedule 
would not fit the 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. rule; examples of 
these are security guards, cook/stewards, cleaners, 
etc.
c) Grievance Handling
I. The Church encourages its employees to bring 
questions, complaints or problems involving 
employment to the attention of their Parish Priest or 
their immediate Supervisor, as the case may be, 
without fear of censure or reprisal.
3231
II. While the matter may not always be resolved to their 
satisfaction, in many cases misunderstandings may 
be cleared and disputes may be resolved.
III. In this policy, the term “grievance” means any 
complaint arising from the interpretation, 
application or claimed violation of the Church's 
employment contract with the individual employee.
d) Discipline
I. The Church enforces discipline in situations of 
misconduct or where work rules are disobeyed.
II. Discipline is necessary to attain Church objectives. 
This includes maintaining a safe and orderly 
workplace, fostering positive and productive 
working relationships, and safeguarding Church 
property and equipment.
III. The primary objective of any disciplinary action is to 
prevent the problem from recurring. Disciplinary 
action is applied in a positive manner that is practical 
as well as equitable and that, as far as possible, 
improves employee morale.
IV. For different infractions and depending on severity, 
the Church would serve a verbal or written warning 
/reprimand, suspend or terminate an employee's 
employment.
V. Care should be taken to distinguish criminal cases 
from civil cases and to take appropriate actions in the 
long - term interest of the individuals and common 
good.
e) Cessation of Employment
I. The Church does not guarantee employment for any 
particular period of time for any employee. An 
employee has the right to end employment with the 
Church at any time with or without cause but with 
notice. Similarly, the Church has the right to 
terminate employment with appropriate notice or 
payment in lieu, with or without cause. The Church 
can end employment by termination, dismissal, 
medical discharge and retirement.
II. Employee can voluntarily end employment if the 
employee:
a. Resigns or retires, with written notice;
b. Elects not to return to work after a leave ends;
c. Absents himself/herself from work for five 
consecutive days without notice to the 
Church or without justification, or both.
6) Performance Management
I. The Church uses a performance management 
process to inform employees of their job 
performance. The process enables the employee in 
conjunction with the Church to plan, manage and 
document job performances of the employee in a 
given year.
II. It requires that at the beginning of the year, both the 
Church and employee would develop performance 
goals and standards that identify the primary areas 
that the employee will focus and deliver results on for 
the Church, as well as identify critical behavioural 
competencies, which describe how to achieve the 
results.
33 34
III. The process also requires periodic review during the 
year to provide guidance and coaching to the 
employee.
IV. At the end of the year, there would be a review of the 
employee's overall year's performance, which would 
be rated on a simple scale of 1 to 5 (1being the 
weakest and 5 the strongest).
7) Compensation and Benefits
a) Salary Determination
The Archdiocese fixes and pays salaries in compliance with 
all applicable Nigerian labour laws administering salaries to 
employees to ensure equity based on job responsibilities and 
job performance and, at the same time, keeps employment 
costs reasonable given the nature of the church as a non-
profit organization.
b) Annual Leave
I. The Church provides for employees to go on paid 
annual vacation as a rest and relaxation time away 
from work.
II. The Church has the right to advance or defer the date 
employee starts vacation if the needs of the church so 
demand. Employees have the responsibility of 
scheduling vacation annually and obtaining 
approvals.
III. Employees earn annual vacation entitlement only 
after completing 12 months of continuous 
employment with the Church. Thereafter, 
employee's vacation accrues as he/she completes a 
calendar year of service.
IV. New employees must complete 12 months of 
continuous service with the Church before they are 
entitled to any vacation.
V. The Church pays eligible employees an annual 
vacation allowance. This allowance is calculated 
using the basic monthly salary in effect when the 
employee commences the vacation.
VI. The number of annual vacation days is based on the 
employee's years of continuous service as shown 
below:
a. Continuous service of less than five years - 
15 work days.
b. Continuous service of five years and above 
- 20 work days
c) Compassionate Leave
I. In consideration of some justifiable personal 
reasons, the Church can grant compassionate leave 
with pay to its employees. Such justifiable reasons 
would typically be based primarily on events 
affecting the requesting employee's immediate 
family (immediate family here is defined as 
employee's spouse, children, parents, grandparents, 
direct parents of employee's spouse, biological 
brothers and sisters).
II. An employee can take up to five workdays in any 
calendar year. These days can only be taken in a 
calendar year; the days of a particular year cannot be 
requested in another year. Employees cannot carry 
over or accumulate compassionate leave days.
35 36
d) Maternity Leave
I. The Church provides maternity benefits for all 
female employees. Maternity Leave shall be 12 
weeks with full pay. In addition to the maternity 
leave, employee will be entitled to annual vacation. 
This means that employee will be entitled to twelve 
weeks maternity leave plus annual vacation of either 
15 or 20 working days, as the case may be.
II. Female employees adopting babies from ages 0–2 
years shall be entitled to the post natal portion of 
maternity leave plus annual vacation. In effect the 
employee will be entitled to six weeks adoption leave 
plus annual vacation of either 15 or 20 working days 
as the case may be.
e) Medical Benefit
I. Employees of the Archdiocese shall be registered 
with reputable hospitals/clinics for medical attention 
as needed. Such attention shall be limited to 
primary/routine interventions as may be needed.
II. A viable medical/health insurance scheme to cover 
all Church employees in the Archdiocese should be 
studied and put in place.
f) Gratuity
I. On leaving the Archdiocesan or Parish employment, 
the staff will be entitled to payment of one month's 
salary for each completed year of service, except in 
the case of summary dismissal.
g) Pension Plan
I. The Archdiocese shall centrally arrange for 
enrolment of all its and parish eligible employees in 
the national contributory pension scheme using one 
Pension Fund Administrator (PFA) to manage the 
entire scheme centrally.
II. The Office of Human Resource Management at the 
Archdiocesan level shall be responsible for the 
management of the scheme on behalf of the 
archdiocese and parishes.
h) Staff Loans
I. Parishes are encouraged to give loans to their staff, 
but not to parishioners. The loan amount should not 
exceed one month's salary, to be repaid over a three-
month period, at equal instalments of not more than 
one-third (1/3) of their monthly salaries.
I.) Parish Welfare Fund
I. Each parish group (society, association, 
organization, etc.) is encouraged to setup and 
manage a welfare fund for assisting those in need.
II. In order to be responsible, accountable, fair and 
transparent, each parish is to encourage a regular 
Parish Welfare Committee meeting where all the 
welfare officers of the parish groups and appropriate 
representatives of all welfare and charitable groups 
in the parish will give a monthly report of all details 
of beneficiaries to the parish designated Welfare 
Organization and receive guidance on prudent 
management of such funds.
8. Human Resource Records & Administration
a) Data Privacy
I. In the normal course of its ecclesial activities, the 
Church collects and processes personal data of 
individuals with whom the Church associates 
(parishioners, employees, vendors etc). These 
37 38
personal data either identify a person or provide 
characteristics of an identifiable person.
II. The Church respects the sensitivity of personal data; 
it is the Church's policy that personal data be 
collected, processed, protected, transferred, stored, 
disclosed and disposed of in accordance with 
applicable laws and Church approved procedures.
III. Employees of the Church, therefore, have a 
responsibility to protect personal data under their 
care from improper use and disclosure. Such data can 
only be disclosed on an officially-determined need 
basis. Accordingly, employees who are unsure of 
their responsibilities shall seek advice from the 
Clergy.
IV. Failure to comply with this policy may result in 
disciplinary action up to and including termination, 
depending on the nature and severity of the violation 
and in accordance with applicable law.
b) Harassment in the Workplace
I. The Church shall strive to provide all employees a 
working environment that is free of harassment 
based on age, disability, ethnic origin, gender, 
political preference, etc. In the light of this, 
harassment of any kind is prohibited among 
employees and between employees and Clergy.
II. The term harassment refers to any unwelcome or 
unsolicited physical, verbal or sexual conduct, either 
explicit or implicit, which by its nature has the effect 
or is calculated to do any of the following:
a. Interfere with an individual's work 
performance or result in creating an 
intimidating, hostile or offensive work 
environment;
b. Result in a change in some condition or term 
of employment;
c. Result in an economic detriment to the 
employee or applicant.
III. Some examples of what may be considered 
harassment, depending on the facts and 
circumstances, include:\
a. Verbal harassment, for example offensive 
jokes, slurs, name calling, display or 
distribution of objectionable cartoons, 
photographs, computer screen images, 
calendars or similar written or graphic items;
b. Physical harassment, for example hitting, 
pushing or other aggressive physical 
conduct, or threats to take such action;
c. Sexual harassment, for example, unwelcome 
or unsolicited sexual advances or 
innuendoes, requests for sexual favors, or 
other verbal or physical conduct of a sexual 
nature. The Archdiocese has a zero-tolerance 
policy on sexual harassment, of which 
proven acts would lead to a summary 
dismissal from employment, or handing over 
to the Police, e.g., in a case of rape.
d. Psychological harassment, for example 
social isolation, exclusion and gang – ups.
39 40
PART D: THE HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT OF 
VOLUNTEERS
1) The Vision of the Laity in the Lagos Catholic Archdiocese
They will be:
· Spiritually – committed
· Following but clear - eyed
· Obedient and humble
· Their brother's keeper
· Correcting in love
2) Volunteering, Assignments and Elections
These guidelines seek to establish clear criteria for lay parish 
leadership and other volunteer positions.
I. Care should be taken to assign meaningful and 
fulfilling tasks and responsibilities to individuals 
based on their competencies and interests in order to 
retain and motivate them.
II. All parishioners should be expected to be cordial and 
welcoming to other parishioners. This should form 
part of the expectations from parishioners stated in 
the parish constitution and given to all members of 
the parish. Where necessary, what this means in 
practical terms will be taught.
III. Each parish is to have clear expectations on how 
volunteers are treated and what they should do when 
aggrieved in order to maintain a positive atmosphere.
IV. Too often conflicts amongst parishioners are left 
unresolved contributing to an unhealthy 
c) Gifts and Conflict of Interest
I. Employees of the Parish or Archdiocese may receive 
gifts of any kind from parishioners or outsiders; such 
shall be treated as personal to the employee. 
However, employees shall not engage in any 
activities that conflict with the Church's interest, 
adversely affects the Church's reputation or 
relationship with others, or interferes with the 
fulfilment of employees' job responsibilities.
II. Employees should perform duties in the best interest 
of the Church at all times. Employees shall not use 
their position, influence, information that can be 
accessed as a result of employment, Church assets or 
resources for personal gain or for the improper 
benefit of others.
III. No employee shall receive any improper payment or 
gratuity from any business enterprise that is a 
supplier or customer of the Archdiocese or its 
affiliates.
41 42
environment and escalations. A free and fair process 
of resolving conflicts is to be established and widely 
communicated at all parishes.
V. Parishes are to establish guidelines and programme 
to help people discover what they do best and give 
them opportunities to contribute in the parish. Efforts 
should be made to create opportunities for every 
member of the parish to be able to contribute.
VI. Efforts should be made to train people where 
necessary and ensure that non – performance and 
underperformance are dealt with expediently and in a 
godly manner so as not to contribute negatively to the 
parish climate.
VII. Each parish is to develop the minimum expectations 
from all parishioners and from parish leaders based 
on Archdiocesan guidelines. This should be 
communicated at every opportunity.
VIII. Each parish or cluster of parishes (where adequate 
human resources are not available within a single 
parish) is to ensure a right fit for all parishioners by 
establishing a GIFTS-SHOP (an office to help 
parishioners identify their spiritual gifts and calling 
and to direct parishioners on their best fit 
volunteer/ministerial options within the parish, 
parish clusters or community in order to actualize 
them). The GIFT SHOP is to be manned by a 
qualified COORDINATOR.
3) Performance Management
I. Parish volunteers and staff are to be guided by an 
Archdiocesan Code of Ethics on good business 
practices, transparency and risk reduction. Parishes 
are to ensure that all staff and volunteers are given an 
orientation on this and that they sign a document that 
they understand them. This is particularly important 
in fund raising, project management and parish 
administration.
II. The Archdiocese requires that all parish leaders and 
volunteers receive on going formation and training to 
ensure their effectiveness in ministry.
III. Parishes are to institute a formal or informal 
evaluation mechanism, as appropriate for all 
volunteers.
IV. The Archdiocesan office of human resource 
management, in conjunction with appropriate 
archdiocesan institutions and lay organizations will 
develop curriculum on various aspects of pastoral 
ministry and leadership competences for the 
effective development of clerical and lay leaders.
4) Re –assignments, Resignations and Expirations of 
Tenures, Relief of Volunteer Positions
I.  Volunteer assignments do come to an end for a 
number of reasons and can be initiated by either the 
volunteer or the parish or institution. Whatever be the 
case, care is to be taken to maintain the integrity and 
dignity of the person. It ought to be done in a spirit of 
love, observing all courtesies (appreciation, 
dialogue, transparency, dignity of the human person 
etc) and taking care to ameliorate the impact on the 
individuals and the community.
43 44
5) Grievance Handling
As a Christian community, we are expected to live in peace 
and harmony always and forgive one another when we are 
wronged. However, if a parishioner is wronged to the point of 
wanting to report the matter to a third party, the following 
shall apply:
a. The wronged parishioner should report the wrong to 
the parish priest;
b. If the parish priest is the one being complained about, 
the complaint should be lodged with the Dean of the 
Deanery.
APPENDICES
Appendix A: Archdiocesan Code of Ethics
All parishes in the Archdiocese of Lagos will commit to the “ 
Standards for Excellence: An Ethics and Accountability Code for 
Catholic Parishes” of The Leadership Roundtable 
(http://www.theleadershiproundtable.org).
Each parish is to obtain a copy of this document and ensure that the 
clergy, staff and lay volunteers are given an orientation with this 
document and where possible that they read and refer to it from time 
to time.
Appendix B: Archdiocesan Expectations from Parishioners and 
Parish Leaders
a. Expectations from Parishioners
As a member of my parish family, I will:
· Attend and participate in Sunday Masses and Holy 
Days of obligations
· Grow in faith and spirituality by participating in 
parish retreats, bible studies and know your faith 
sessions.
· Share time, talent and treasure (resources).
· Be welcoming to fellow parishioners and new 
comers.
· Participate in Small Christian Communities
· Participate in other parish social activities.
b. Expectations from Parish Leaders
Parish staff, clergy and ministry/committee leaders will:
· Inspire spirituality
· Teach the Catholic faith
· Cultivate an open and welcoming atmosphere
· Be good stewards of parishioners' gifts of time, 
treasure and talents.

Archdiocese of Lagos Archdiocesan Laity Council Guidelines   
2  
Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	Guidelines	1) Overview	The	Laity	 Council	 is	 charged	 by	 the	 Decree	 on	 the	 Apostolate	 of	 the	 Laity	 (Apostolicam	Actuositatem)	with	the	goal	of	intensifying	the	apostolic	activity	of	the	laity	by	promoting	the	apostolate	and	coordinating	lay	initiatives.		While	Deanery	Laity	Councils	and	Archdiocesan	groups	and	organizations	are	responsible	for	organizing	and	directing	their	own	activities,	the	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	is	charged	with	promoting	 solidarity	and	mutual	 assistance	 amongst	 Deanery	Laity	 Councils	 and	Archdiocesan	lay	organizations,	in	their	tasks	of	coordinating	and	promoting	lay	apostolic	zeal	amongst	Catholics,	lay	organisations	and	groups	in	the	Archdiocese.		2) The	Purpose	and	Functions	of	the	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	Promoting	 Solidarity	and	 providing	 mutual	 assistance	 amongst	 deaneries	and	archdiocesan	organizations	in	carrying	out	their	promotional	and	coordinating	roles	as	itemized	below:	Promotional	a. Promote	existing	 and	 new	lay	apostolate	initiatives	 with	 the	 goal	 of	 educating	parishioners	and	getting	them	more	involved	in	the	apostolate.		b. Research	 the	 needs	 of	 the	 archdiocese	and	 propose	 the	 introduction	of	 new	 lay	initiatives	to	the	Deanery	Laity	Councils	(	DLCs	)	and	the	Archbishop.	c. Encourage	and	support	DLCs	and	Archdiocesan	organizations	to	run	programs	that	help	the	laity	discover	their	spiritual	gifts	and	talents	thereby	empowering	them	for	apostolic	work.				d. Support	DLCS	in	their	evaluations	of	proposals	for	new	apostolic	initiatives	and	make	recommendations	on	their	proper	pastoral	classification	and	fit.		e. Promote	apostolate	formation	such	as	ensuring	that	all	Council	members	mandatorily	participate	 in	the	Leading	 for	 Mission	 training	 programmes	 and	 organizing	instructions	in	 theology,	 ethics	 and	 philosophy,	 culture	and	good	 human	 relations,	based	on	identified	needs.						
3  
Coordination	a. Carry	out	research	and	study	in	the	apostolate	field	and	on	specific	lay	and	human	ethical	and	religious	issues	that	pose	a	danger	to	Christian	life.	b. Discern	 the	 particular	 and	 common	 needs	 of	 lay	 groups	 with	 similar	 areas	 of	apostolate	 for	 the	 purposes	 of	 advice,	and	 provide	resources	for	 the	 formation	 of	individuals	and	leaders	for	intensified	apostolic	activities.		c. Deal	with	unexpected	crisis,	obstacles	or	interruptions	and	efficiently	get	things	back	on	track	and	prevent	further	crises.			d. Assess	the	apostolic	developmental	needs	of	the	archdiocese	and	fill	the	needs.			e. Develop	 and	 implement	 an	 annual	 calendar	 of	 leadership,	 management	 and	 social	skills	 development	 programmes	 for	enhancing	the	effectiveness	of	DLC	 and	archdiocesan	organization	executives	as	leaders.		3) Core	Areas	for	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	Operations	Coordinating	 inter-deanery	and	 inter–archdiocesan	group	 evaluations	 with	 the	 view	 to	incentivising	growth	in	the	effectiveness	of	deaneries	and	archdiocesan	organizations.		a. Support	 DLCs	 and	 Archdiocesan	Organizations	 in	 their	execution	 of	 relevant	programmes	 aimed	 at	 equipping	 and	 strengthening	 the	 apostolic	 capacity	 of	members.		b. Support	DLCs	and	Archdiocesan	Organizations	in	the	effective	execution	of	an	annual	or	 bi–annual	 programme	 aimed	 at	 promoting	 existing	 and	 new	 lay	 apostolic	initiatives	as	needed	in	the	parishes.		c. Support	DLCs	and	Archdiocesan	Organizations	in	the	effective	and	timely	resolution	of	conflicts	amongst	lay	initiatives.		d. Encourage	and	 evaluate	spiritual	growth	 in	 the	 deaneries	 and	 archdiocesan	organizations	through	programmes	to	assist	the	laity	to	find	and	live	their	callings	through	effective	talents	/	spiritual	gifts	discovery	and	development	programmes.		e. Evaluate	the	outcomes	of	annual	parish	and	organizational	engagement	surveys	to	determine	model	deaneries	and	organizations	in	the	archdiocese.			f. Develop	and	coordinate	an	election	timetable	for	member	organizations.		4) The	Composition	of	Archdiocesan	Pastoral	Committees	The	following	associations,	ministries,	organizations	and	societies	are	listed	to	assist	in	their	classification	into	relevant	pastoral	committees.	Their	existence	in	parishes,	deaneries	and	the	archdiocese	is	based	on	discerned	needs	and	approvals	from	relevant	authorities.		
4  
a. Liturgical	 Committee	-	It	 is	 responsible	 for	 coordinating	 the	 planning,	implementation	and	evaluation	of	Archdiocesan	liturgies,	under	the	direction	of	the	Archbishop	 and	the	 Archdiocesan	 Liturgy	 Committee.	 	 The	Committee	oversees	 all	activities	associated	with	public	worship	(liturgies).	Members	of	the	Committee	are	two	designated	officers	of:	a. Altar	Servers	b. Lectors	c. The	Choir	d. The	Church	Wardens	e. Altar	Decorators		f. Sacristan		g. Etc			b. Faith	 Formation	 and	 Education	 Committee	–	It	coordinates	the	 planning,	implementation	and	evaluation	of	deanery	faith	formation	and	education	including	family	life	education	for	all	age	groups	and	families.	The	Committee	consists	of	lay	groups	whose	main	charism	involves	assisting	individuals	to	grow	in	their	catholic	faith,	 character,	 discipleship	 and	 apostleship.	Members	of	 the	 Committee	are	 two	designated	officers	of:		 i. Association	of	catechists		ii. RCIA	facilitators	iii. Family	Life	Ministry	facilitators	iv. Marriage	Preparation	facilitators	v. Catholic	Charismatic	Renewal	vi. Catholic	Evangelizers	vii. Schools	of	Evangelisation	viii. The	Renew	ix. The	Focolare	x. Communion	and	Liberation	xi. Catholic	Biblical	Instructors	Union	xii. Devotional	Committee	–	It	 coordinates	the	 planning,	 implementation	 and	evaluation	of	the	activities	of	the	societies,	associations	and	groups	whose	primary	charism	 is	 prayerful	 devotions.	Members	of	 the	 Committee	are	 two	designated	officers	of:

Community	Building	Committee	–	It	consists	of	the	structured	efforts	to	promote	the	 spirit	 of	 community	 in	 the	 parish	 through	 Small	 Christian	 Communities	 and	 in	communities	based	on	residence,	gender,	age	and	profession	who	come	together	to	solve	 their	 common	 problems	 in	 and	 outside	 the	 parish	 or	to	 contribute	 their	professional	expertise	to	be	more	effective	witnesses	of	Christ	in	their	corners	of	the	world. Members	of	the	Committee	are	two	designated	officers	of:				 i. JDPC	ii. Pastoral	Bereavement	and	Caring	Ministry	iii. Society	of	St	Vincent	De	Paul		iv. Madonna	Mercy		v. The	Catholic	Workers	Volunteer	Force	vi. The	Red	Cross	Society	vii. Knights	&	Ladies	of	St	Mulumba		viii. Knights	&	Ladies	of	St	John	International	ix. Association	of	Papal	Knights	and	Medallists.		x. Etc.		f. Evangelization	 Committee	–	receives	 its	 general	 mandate	 from	 the	 Archdiocesan	Evangelization	 Commission,	 and	promotes	 and	 coordinates	 all	 parish	 and	 deanery	evangelization	activities	and	initiatives.	4.1.	Archdiocesan	Associations,	Ministries,	Societies	and	Organizations	The	activities	of	the	associations,	ministries,	societies	and	organizations	listed	in	4a–4f	above	achieve	 their	 full	 expression	 at	 the	 parish	 level.	 But	 each	 of	 these	 organizations	 is	represented	at	 the	 deanery	 level	 by	 three	 (3)	 members	 only	who,	 together	 with	representatives	 from	 other	 parishes,	constitute	 the	 association,	 ministry,	 society	 and	organization,	as	the	case	may	be,	at	the	deanery	level.	Similarly,	each	of	these	deanery	level	organizations	will	be	represented	at	the	archdiocesan	level	 by	two	 (2)	 members	 only,	 who	 will	join	 representatives	 from	 other	 deaneries	 to	constitute	 the	 association,	 ministry,	 society	 or	 organization,	 as	 the	 case	 may	 be,	 at	 the	archdiocesan	level.

	4.2.	Archdiocesan	Pastoral	Committees	Each	 archdiocesan	 level	 association,	 ministry,	 society	 and	 organization	 will	 send	two	 (2)	representatives	to	the	Archdiocesan	Pastoral	Committee	to	which	it	properly	belongs,	that	is,	 Liturgical,	 Faith	 Formation	&	Education,	 Devotional,	 Community	 Building,	 Welfare	 &	Social	Action,	and	Evangelization,	as	the	case	may	be.	5) Membership		The	Archdiocesan	Laity	 Council	 is	 an	 assembly	 of	 the	 chairpersons	 and	 secretaries	 of	all	Deanery	Laity	Councils	(40),	one	representative	from	each	of	the	six	Archdiocesan	Pastoral	Committees	(6),	and	one	representative	each	from	the	Knights	and	Ladies	of	St	Mulumba,	the	 Knights	 and	Ladies	 of	 St	 John	 International,	 and	 the	 Association	 of	 Papal	 Knights	 and	Medallists	(3),	for	a	total	of	49	members.		6) Officers	(Executive	Committee)	The	 officers	 of	 the	Archdiocesan	Laity	 Council,	 and	of	all	 other	 archdiocesan	 level	organizations	and	groups,	shall	be:		i. Chairperson	ii. Vice-Chairperson		iii. General	Secretary	iv. Assistant	General	Secretary	v. Treasurer	vi. Financial	 Secretary:		will	 collate	 and	 monitor	 the	 financial	 reports	 of	 all	member	organisations.	vii. Welfare	Officer:	collates	and	coordinates	the	monthly	reports	of	the	welfare	activities	of	all	member	groups	through	their	respective	Welfare	Officers.		viii. Assistant	Welfare	Officer	ix. Provost	x. The	Co-ordinator	of	each	of	the	five	Committees	of	the	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council.	The	Coordinator	of	the	Publicity	Committee	assumes	the	role	of	the	PRO/Publicity	Secretary).		The	officers	shall	be	elected	from	the	membership	of	the	Council.	7) Tenure	The	tenure	of	the	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	is	four	(4)	years,	non-renewable,	after	which	the	 members	 will	 not	 be	 eligible	 for	 re-election	under	 any	 guise	 until	 after	 one	 term	 has	elapsed.		Only	members	of	the	Council	can	vote	and	be	voted	for.

			8) Frequency	of	Meetings		It	is	recommended	that	the	archdiocesan	organizations	and	groups	meet	quarterly	prior	to	the	quarterly	Archdiocesan	Laity	 Council	meetings.	Archdiocesan	 Committees	 may	 meet	quarterly	to	plan	events.		9) Relationships	Relationship	 to	 the	 Archbishop,	Archdiocesan	 Director	 of	 Lay	 Apostolate,	Cathedral	Administrator,	Archdiocesan	Pastoral	Council	and	Archdiocesan	Finance	Council	a. The	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	works	 collaboratively	with	 the	 Archbishop,	Archdiocesan	 Pastoral	 and	 Finance	 Councils	 and	 all	 Archdiocesan	bodies	and	organizations.		b. The	 ALC	 takes	 its	 general	 mandate	 from	 the	Archdiocesan	 Director	 of	 Lay	Apostolate	 but	 must	 take	 its	 specific	 goals	 from	 the	 needs	 of	 the	 Archdiocesan	Pastoral	Council,	the	Deanery	Laity	Councils	and	the	Archdiocesan	organizations	that	it	serves.		Relationship	with	Deanery	Laity	Councils	a. The	Archdiocesan	Laity	Council	exists	to	provide	support	and	coordination,	and	for	 promoting	 solidarity	 and	 mutual	 assistance,	among	 the	Deanery	Laity	Councils.	b. The	Deanery	Laity	Councils	will	each	have	two	representatives	at	the	ALC	and	will	report	regularly	to	it.		c. The	DLC	will	render	quarterly	reports	and	recommendations	to	the	ALC	to	assist	it	in	its	planning	role.			Relationship	with	Archdiocesan	Lay	Organizations	and	Groups	a. The	ALC	 exists	 to	 provide	 support	 and	coordination,	 and	to	promote	solidarity	and	mutual	assistance,	among	the	Archdiocesan	groups	and	organizations.		b. The	ALC	needs	to	be	aware	of	all	archdiocesan	organization	and	group	activities;	however,	it	is	not	within	the	purview	of	the	ALC	to	direct	their	activities.	c. The	ALC	provides	advisory	and	supportive	services	to	the	DLCs	and	archdiocesan	groups.		d. The	 ALC	 funds	 its	 activities	principally	from	 its	 20%	 share	 of	 the	 5th	Sunday	collections	in	the	Archdiocese	and,	secondarily,	through	rates	and	donations	for	valuable	services	provided	to	the	Deanery	Laity	Councils	and	archdiocesan	level	organizations	and	groups.
